frame_analyses.description: Setup view — Penrose drain on red foam suture block. Standard FLS Task 5 configuration. No instruments visible.
At 28 seconds, both instruments in field working on the drain. Suture visible with needle being driven through drain tissue. Instruments actively engaged close to the drain surface.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture placement in progress. Instruments appear coordinated. Needle driving technique visible.

frame_analyses.description: At 74 seconds, both instruments at drain with suture loop visible near the surface. Still working on completing suture placement or transitioning to first throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Extended time in suture placement phase — 74 seconds elapsed and potentially still placing suture. This is longer than post-2 (suture placement completed by ~58s). May indicate difficulty with needle passage through the second mark.

frame_analyses.description: At 93 seconds, suture wrapping visible around the left instrument. Both instruments positioned with suture going through tissue and looping above. Classic throw wrapping configuration.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: First throw wrapping in progress. The surgeon's knot (double throw) pattern appears to be forming with the suture looped around the left instrument.

frame_analyses.description: At 111 seconds, left instrument holds suture loop, right instrument holding free end. Suture thread visible. Wrapping for second throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Second throw in progress. The instrument positions suggest wrapping, though hand switch is difficult to confirm definitively from this angle.

frame_analyses.description: At 148 seconds, both instruments working close to drain surface. Suture thread visible extending upward. The right instrument appears to have a different profile — possibly scissors have been introduced.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Late in the throw phase or transitioning to cutting. The timing suggests this could be the tail end of the third throw or early cutting.

frame_analyses.description: At 176 seconds, both instruments still engaged near drain. Left instrument with suture, right instrument appears to be scissors. Suture cutting in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture cutting phase is taking significant time — instruments still engaged at 176 seconds.

frame_analyses.description: Final frame at 185 seconds. Instruments withdrawn. Completed knot visible on Penrose drain with dark suture thread at knot site. The drain slit shows a SMALL VISIBLE GAP — the upper portion of the slit is not fully approximated, though the area around the knot is partially closed. Drain remains attached to suture block (not avulsed).
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Final result shows partial slit closure with a small residual gap. This is better than the pre-training video but not as good as post-practice or post-2. The knot appears present but the gap suggests either insufficient tension on throws or slight suture placement deviation.

knot_assessments.notes: Frame 11 shows wrapping around left instrument consistent with surgeon's knot. Confidence moderate.
Frame 13 shows throw wrapping. Instrument positions suggest hand change from throw 1. Confidence low — camera angle limits visibility.
Third throw completed but final result shows a residual gap in the drain slit, suggesting the knot construct may not be providing sufficient closure tension. Confidence low.

technique_summary: This fourth video shows a slight regression in performance compared to post-2, though it remains improved over the pre-training baseline. Completion time of ~180 seconds is slower than post-2 (158s) and post-practice (170s). The primary time loss is in the suture placement phase, which extended to approximately 60 seconds — significantly longer than the 40 seconds achieved in the previous two post-training videos. The throw phases were actually faster (22-27 seconds each) suggesting improved knot tying mechanics, but the suture cutting phase was extended at ~30 seconds. The final result shows a small visible gap in the drain slit, indicating that the improvements in closure quality seen in post-2 were not maintained. All three throws were completed and the drain was not avulsed. This may represent normal performance variability during the learning curve.
improvement_suggestions: Suture placement regressed to 60 seconds — focus on consistent needle driving technique to maintain the 40-second pace achieved in previous attempts
Suture cutting took ~30 seconds which is the longest of all post-training attempts — practice efficient scissors introduction and cutting
The gap in the slit closure suggests knot tension needs attention — ensure each throw is tightened before proceeding to the next
Performance variability is normal during skill acquisition — continue practicing to build consistency
strengths: Throw phases were individually faster than previous attempts (22-27s each vs 25-30s)
Needle loading maintained at efficient 13 seconds
All three throws completed in correct sequence
Drain not avulsed — consistent good tissue handling
Overall time (180s) still significantly better than pre-training baseline (198s)

key_observations: Drain closure regressed: complete (post-2) → partial gap (post-3)
Normal variability expected during learning — overall trend still positive from baseline
